summary: Global markets are gripped by risk-off sentiment as escalating conflict involving Iran drives world shares lower while oil prices surge sharply higher, with falling US crude inventories and strong export demand amplifying supply concerns. The dominant macro driver is the ongoing Iran war, with the US House voting to end hostilities in a rebuke to Trump, while Trump claims Iran has agreed to forgo nuclear weapons and an interim deal appears possible, though Hezbollah rocket launches at Israel signal continued regional instability. In equities, Broadcom slipped on a revenue miss, Palo Alto Networks faced a post-earnings sell-off raising questions about cybersecurity valuations, and Honeywell Aerospace is generating optimism ahead of its standalone debut, while AI-linked chipmakers continue to attract institutional buying interest. Key risks investors are monitoring include potential Strait of Hormuz disruption — with the EU proposing a naval mission to clear mines — surging European energy prices that the EU Commission warns could cost 1.3 million jobs, a cooling US housing market with home listings being pulled at the fastest pace since 2020, and political uncertainty surrounding US fiscal legislation. The short-term outlook remains cautious and volatile; the historic equity rally faces a critical test as geopolitical risk premiums stay elevated, oil prices pressure corporate margins globally, and resolution of the Iran conflict remains uncertain despite diplomatic signals.
